30	\section{Setup and motivation}
31	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
32	
33	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
34	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
35	(\cref{def:genus}). By
36	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
37	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
38	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
39	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
40	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
41	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
42	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
43	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
44	degree of a line bundle.
45	
46	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
47	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
48	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
49	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
50	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
51	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
52	
53	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
54	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
55	
56	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
57	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
58	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
59	Lean substrate
60	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
61	outside the Picard context.
62	
63	\begin{definition}\leanok
64	[Identity component of a group scheme]
65	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
67	  \uses{def:identity_component_carrier}
68	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
69	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
70	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
71	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
72	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
73	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
74	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
77	  %
78	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
79	  % reduced open subscheme.
80	  %
81	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
82	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
83	  % extending \(k\).
84	  %  \end{lem}"
85	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
86	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
87	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
88	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
89	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
90	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
91	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
92	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
93	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
94	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
95	\end{definition}

106	% \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.

109	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
110	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
111	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
112	
113	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
114	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
115	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
116	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

118	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with

120	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
121	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
122	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
123	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
124	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
125	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
126	\end{theorem}

132	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly

134	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus

162	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the

184	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}

187	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
188	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
189	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over

193	\begin{proof}
194	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
195	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (subgroup part); the
196	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
197	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
198	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
199	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
200	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
201	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
202	  image lies in the clopen identity component \(G^0\)
203	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence \(G^0\) is closed
204	  under the group operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)):
205	  the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of
206	  \(k\)-group schemes, and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme
207	  structure from \(G\).
208	\end{proof}

213	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
214	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}

222	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
223	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
224	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
225	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
226	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
227	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
228	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
229	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
230	\end{theorem}

232	\begin{proof}
233	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
234	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (finite-type and
235	  geometric-irreducibility part); the verbatim source quote is
236	  reproduced at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
237	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
238	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
239	  commutes with field extension,
240	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Over
241	  \(\overline k\), the product
242	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
243	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
244	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
245	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
246	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
247	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
248	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
249	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
250	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
251	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
252	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
253	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
254	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).

261	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
262	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
263	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section, lem:identity_component_geometrically_connected, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
264	
265	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
266	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
267	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
268	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
270	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
271	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
272	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
273	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
274	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
275	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
276	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
277	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
278	\end{theorem}

280	\begin{proof}
281	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section, lem:identity_component_geometrically_connected, def:identity_component_group_scheme}
282	  Following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (base-change part); the
283	  verbatim source quote is reproduced at
284	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
285	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
286	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\): the identity component is
287	  connected and carries a \(k\)-rational section, so it is geometrically
288	  connected by
289	  \cref{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section} (the
290	  Stacks 037Q criterion; cf.\ EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). In particular for
291	  any field extension \(K/k\) the base change \((G^0)_K\) is connected,
292	  contains the image of the identity section of \(G_K\), and is open and
293	  closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and closed-immersion both descend under
294	  base change, \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}). Hence
295	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
296	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
297	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
298	\end{proof}

322	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}

326	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).

328	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
329	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)

335	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
336	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
337	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
338	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
339	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
340	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
341	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
342	  connected.
343	\end{lemma}
344	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
345	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
346	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
347	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
348	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
349	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
350	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
351	% ring is a field is connected.''

353	  % DAG iter-266: removed spurious \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
354	  % — the Stacks 037Q/04KV argument uses only connectedness of |X| and the
355	  % k-rational section, not the identity-component clopen theorem.
356	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
357	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
358	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
359	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
360	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
361	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
362	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
363	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
364	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
365	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
366	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
367	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
368	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
369	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
370	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
371	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
372	  is therefore connected.

391	\subsection{Topological and connectedness substrate (helper lemmas)}
392	\label{subsec:identity_component_substrate}
393	
394	The construction of \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} and the
395	proofs of its open-and-closed and base-change conclusions rest on a small
396	chain of helper results: a purely topological core about connected
397	components of Noetherian spaces, the identity point and its connected
398	component (the carrier of \(G^0\)), the local connectedness of \(|G|\), the
399	identity section landing in \(G^0\), and the connectedness of \(G^0\). We
400	record each helper so the dependency graph of the chapter is faithful to
401	the Lean development.
402	
403	\begin{lemma}
404	[Finitely many connected components on a Noetherian space]
405	  \label{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}
406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents}
407	  Let \(\alpha\) be a Noetherian topological space. Then the set of
408	  connected components of \(\alpha\) is finite.
409	\end{lemma}
410	\begin{proof}
411	  The assignment sending an irreducible component \(C\) of \(\alpha\) to the
412	  connected component of an arbitrarily chosen point of \(C\) is
413	  well-defined (an irreducible component is preconnected, hence lies in a
414	  single connected component) and surjective (every point lies in the
415	  irreducible component through it, which is contained in its connected
416	  component). A Noetherian space has finitely many irreducible components,
417	  so the connected components are finite as the image of a finite set.
418	  Proved directly in Lean.
419	\end{proof}
420	
421	\begin{lemma}
422	[Connected components are open on a Noetherian space]
423	  \label{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent}
425	  \uses{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}
426	  Let \(\alpha\) be a Noetherian topological space and \(x \in \alpha\). Then
427	  the connected component \(\connComp(x)\) of \(x\) is open in \(\alpha\).
428	\end{lemma}
429	\begin{proof}
430	  \uses{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}
431	  The space of connected components of \(\alpha\) is totally disconnected,
432	  hence \(T_1\), and finite by
433	  \cref{lem:noetherian_finite_connected_components}; a finite \(T_1\) space
434	  carries the discrete topology, so each singleton is open. The preimage of
435	  the singleton \(\{\,[x]\,\}\) under the continuous quotient map
436	  \(\alpha \to \pi_0(\alpha)\) is exactly \(\connComp(x)\), which is
437	  therefore open. Proved directly in Lean.
438	\end{proof}
439	
440	\begin{lemma}
441	[The underlying space of a locally-finite-type \(k\)-scheme is locally connected]
442	  \label{lem:identity_component_locally_connected}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace}
444	  \uses{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component}
445	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) an object of the over-category over
446	  \(\Spec k\) whose structural morphism \(G \to \Spec k\) is locally of
447	  finite type. Then the underlying topological space \(|G|\) is locally
448	  connected.
449	\end{lemma}
450	\begin{proof}
451	  \uses{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component}
452	  Being locally of finite type over the field \(k\), the scheme \(G\) is
453	  locally Noetherian. Each point \(y \in |G|\) has an affine open
454	  neighbourhood \(W = \Spec R\) with \(R\) Noetherian, so \(|W|\) is a
455	  Noetherian space; by
456	  \cref{lem:noetherian_isopen_connected_component} the connected component
457	  of \(y\) inside \(W\) is open, and its image under the open inclusion
458	  \(W \hookrightarrow |G|\) is an open connected neighbourhood of \(y\)
459	  contained in any prescribed open set through \(y\). This is precisely the
460	  local-connectedness criterion. Proved directly in Lean.
461	\end{proof}

465	  \label{def:identity_section_point}
466	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.identitySectionPoint}

468	  \emph{identity point} of \(G\) is the image \(e(\ast) \in |G|\) of the
469	  unique point of \(\Spec k\) under the identity section
470	  \(e : \Spec k \to G\) (well-defined because \(|\Spec k|\) is a
471	  one-point space).
472	\end{definition}
473	\begin{proof}
474	  Proved directly in Lean (a one-point evaluation of the identity
475	  section).
476	\end{proof}

501	  \label{lem:identity_component_carrier_connected}

535	[The identity section lands in the identity-component carrier]

553	  \label{def:identity_component_section}

659	\section{The degree map}
660	\label{sec:pic_degree}
661	
662	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
663	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
664	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
665	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
666	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
667	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
668	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
669	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
670	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
671	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
672	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
673	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
674	degree.
675	
676	\begin{definition}
677	[Degree of a Picard class]
678	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
679	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
680	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:hilbert_polynomial, thm:fga_pic_representability}
681	
682	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
683	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
684	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
687	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
688	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
689	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
690	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
691	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
692	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
693	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
694	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
695	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
696	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
697	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
698	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
699	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
700	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
701	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
702	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
703	  \[
704	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
705	  \]
706	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
707	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
708	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
709	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
710	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
711	  \[
712	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
713	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
714	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
715	  \]
716	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
717	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
718	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
719	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
720	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
721	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
722	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
723	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
724	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
725	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
726	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
727	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
728	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
729	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
730	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
731	  of degree \(d\).
732	\end{definition}

734	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
735	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
736	
737	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
738	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
739	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
740	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
741	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
742	automatically.

811	\begin{proof}
812	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism, thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible, thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes, thm:fga_pic_representability, thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
813	  We assemble the four conjuncts of the abelian-variety structure on
814	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) from four inputs.
815	
816	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
817	  By
818	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
819	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
820	  type by
821	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
822	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
823	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
824	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
825	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
826	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
827	
828	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
829	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
830	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
831	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
832	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
833	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
834	
835	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
836	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
837	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
838	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
839	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
840	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
841	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
842	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
843	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
844	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
845	
846	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
847	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
848	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
849	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
850	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
851	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
852	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
853	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
854	
855	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
856	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
857	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
858	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
859	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
860	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
861	\end{proof}

923	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:pic_zero_subscheme, def:divisor_degree_pic}
924

929	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
930	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
931	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
932	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
933	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
934	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
935	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
936	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
937	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
938	  if and only if the degree
939	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
940	  vanishes. Equivalently,

944	\begin{proof}
945	  \uses{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety, thm:identity_component_open_subgroup, def:pic_zero_subscheme, def:divisor_degree_pic}
946	  A class
947	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
948	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
949	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
950	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, specialised to
951	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) --- precisely when
952	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
953	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
954	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
955	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the
956	  abelian variety of \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}, this image
957	  is exactly the degree-zero locus on \(k\)-points. Hence
958	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
959	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
960	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
961	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
962	  zero on \(C\)'').
963	\end{proof}
